meta.title: Ophthalmology Practice Software | Cataract Coordination & Retina Injection Management | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to ophthalmology practice operations. Cataract surgery coordination at scale. OCT and visual field test tracking. Anti-VEGF injection scheduling. Dilation-aware high-volume patient flow.

hero.subhead: Cataract surgery at 30-40 cases per week needs systematic pre-op coordination. Anti-VEGF injection programs with 300+ patients need scheduling management that prevents missed treatments. High-volume clinic flow with dilation windows needs real-time patient tracking. clinIQ manages the operational complexity of modern ophthalmology.

stats.label: higher cataract surgical volume through better coordination

sections.content:
Ophthalmology operates at a scale and complexity that most practice management systems weren't designed for. A busy ophthalmology practice sees 50-80 patients per day per provider, runs diagnostic testing that precedes every patient encounter, manages surgical volume that fills ASC schedules weeks in advance, and tracks injection programs where missed treatments directly cause vision loss.

The operational challenges stack:

• Cataract surgery requires 8-12 pre-operative coordination steps per patient — biometry, IOL selection, medical clearance, facility scheduling, implant confirmation — multiplied by 30-40 cases per week
• Retina practices manage 200-400 patients on anti-VEGF injection schedules. Drug-specific intervals vary (4 weeks, 8 weeks, 12 weeks). A patient who misses an Eylea injection develops macular degeneration progression that may never fully reverse
• Every patient visit is preceded by diagnostic testing — OCT, visual fields, fundus photography, tonometry — that must be completed before the provider sees the patient. Without coordination, testing backs up and providers wait
• Dilation requires a 20-45 minute window during which patients are in the practice but not in an exam room. Managing patient flow around dilation windows without either creating waiting room jams or leaving exam rooms empty requires real-time visibility

These aren't small inefficiencies. A cataract practice that increases surgical volume by 15% through better pre-op coordination increases revenue by hundreds of thousands annually. A retina practice with 5% injection non-compliance has patients losing vision unnecessarily.

Cataract surgery is the most commonly performed surgical procedure in the United States — and for busy ophthalmology practices, the operational demands of managing high surgical volume are among the most significant constraints on practice growth. A practice that can coordinate 40 cataract cases per week efficiently can grow; one that's limited by administrative bottlenecks cannot.

**The cataract surgery coordination sequence:**

Surgical decision at clinic visit: Patient counseled on surgical candidacy, lens options (monofocal, toric, premium IOL), and expectations. Surgical consent obtained or scheduled.

Biometry and IOL calculation: A-scan measurements, IOL Master or Lenstar data, IOL selection using Barrett Universal II or other formula. Advanced biometry for toric and premium lens candidates.

Medical clearance: For patients with cardiac disease, pulmonary conditions, or anticoagulation — coordination with PCP or specialist. Labs if required.

Facility scheduling: ASC or hospital OR time reserved. Many practices have block time at ASCs — slots must be filled efficiently. Open slots cost revenue; overbooked slots create day-of cancellations.

Implant ordering: IOL selected, implant ordered from manufacturer or distributor. Confirmation received. Backup IOL options confirmed.

Pre-operative instructions: Patient education, drop protocol, day-of arrival instructions, driver arrangement.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Surgical pipeline: Every patient scheduled for surgery with pre-operative checklist completion status. Biometry complete, IOL selected and ordered, clearance obtained, facility confirmed, consent signed, pre-op instructions sent. Cases not cleared for surgery are flagged before the schedule is finalized.

IOL inventory: Implants ordered, expected delivery date, confirmation received. Case cancelled for missing IOL — never again.

Post-operative schedule: 1-day, 1-week, 1-month visits scheduled before patient leaves surgery.

**Related: [Cataract Surgery Coordination: Pre-Op to Post-Op at Scale](/blog/cataract-surgery-coordination)**

Retina practices managing anti-VEGF therapy face a scheduling coordination problem at scale. 300 active injection patients. Drug-specific intervals. Prior authorization requirements per drug. Patients who miss injections risk permanent vision loss. The operational stakes are high.

**Drug-specific injection intervals:**

Aflibercept (Eylea): Every 4 weeks for loading doses, then every 8 weeks for maintenance. Eylea HD: Every 8-16 weeks after loading.

Ranibizumab (Lucentis): Every 4 weeks typically.

Bevacizumab (Avastin): Every 4 weeks — off-label but widely used; compounded from oncology vials.

Faricimab (Vabysmo): Every 4 weeks for loading doses, then extended to 8-16 weeks based on response.

Brolucizumab (Beovu): Every 6 weeks for loading, then every 8-12 weeks based on response.

**The scheduling management problem:**

With 300 injection patients on varying intervals, scheduling is a dynamic puzzle. Patients due in the same week compete for clinic slots. Patients who call to reschedule may push into the next drug window. Patients who miss appointments may not realize the clinical stakes.

Payer authorization adds complexity: Eylea, Lucentis, and Vabysmo all require prior authorization, typically renewed every 12 months. Authorization expiration must not interrupt treatment.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Injection program dashboard: Every injection patient visible with last injection date, drug, next due date, and scheduling status. Color-coded: On schedule (green), due soon (yellow), overdue (red).

Proactive scheduling: Patient due in 3 weeks, not yet scheduled → outreach initiated. Patient approaching due date without appointment → alert to scheduling staff.

Authorization management: Auth status per drug per patient. Renewal triggered 30 days before expiration. Treatment not interrupted by authorization lapse.

Bilateral coordination: Patients with injections needed in both eyes — bilateral scheduling coordinated on the same day visit.

Ophthalmology generates more diagnostic testing per visit than almost any specialty. Every patient encounter is preceded by one or more tests. Managing this testing volume efficiently — ensuring the right test is done before the patient reaches the exam room, results are available when the provider needs them, and testing doesn't create bottlenecks that back up the clinic — is an operational challenge at high volumes.

**Testing volume by visit type:**

Routine comprehensive exam: Visual acuity, refraction, tonometry, fundus exam. Optional: OCT (baseline screening), fundus photography.

Glaucoma monitoring: Visual fields (Humphrey), OCT RNFL, corneal thickness. Baseline and monitoring frequency based on disease severity and progression rate.

Macular degeneration monitoring: OCT macula at every visit. Fluorescein angiography for active leakage assessment. Fundus autofluorescence.

Cataract pre-op: Biometry (A-scan/optical), corneal topography (for toric IOL), endothelial cell count (Specular microscopy) for high-risk patients.

Diabetic eye disease: Fundus photography for diabetic retinopathy screening. OCT for diabetic macular edema. Fluorescein angiography for proliferative disease.

**The testing coordination problem:**

Technology rooms with OCT machines, visual field units, and cameras have limited capacity. Multiple patients arriving simultaneously create queues. Patients who should have visual fields done before their glaucoma follow-up don't get scheduled with adequate time. Providers wait for testing to finish before they can see patients.

**What clinIQ coordinates:**

Pre-visit testing order: Protocol-based testing orders generated automatically based on visit type. Patient arrives knowing what testing is needed before entering the exam lane. Technician queue: Testing staff see a prioritized queue of who needs what test, in what order. Results availability: Testing results automatically available in chart when provider opens the encounter. Bottleneck detection: OCT queue backing up → alert to add technician capacity.

Dilation is the defining challenge of ophthalmology patient flow. When a patient is dilated, they need 20-45 minutes before the provider can complete the fundus examination. During that window, patients are in the practice but not in exam rooms — and exam rooms sit empty while patients wait in dilated waiting areas or chairs.

**The dilation flow problem:**

Without dilation tracking, providers don't know when dilated patients are ready. The MA who dilated a patient may have moved to another room. The provider doesn't know who has been waiting 45 minutes and who was dilated 10 minutes ago. Exam rooms are occupied by patients who aren't ready, while ready patients wait.

The result: inefficient use of provider time, extended patient wait times, and compressed end-of-session where providers rush through patients who have been waiting too long.

**The dilation window as a workflow asset:**

With proper management, the dilation window becomes an opportunity. While one patient is dilating, another patient is being examined. Technicians perform pre-testing on the next patient while the provider completes the exam on the current one. The dilation window, managed correctly, creates workflow parallelism that increases the number of patients seen per session.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Dilation timestamp: Time of dilation recorded when drops are instilled. Provider notification: Alert when dilation window is complete for each patient — no more provider asking 'is room 3 ready?'

Dilating vs. ready status: Queue shows which patients are dilating (with elapsed time) and which are ready for provider examination. Provider can see at a glance which room to go to next.

Flow sequencing: Patients can be optimally sequenced — go to undilated patient while dilated patient's window is completing. Reduce idle time between patients.

Dilation protocol tracking: Which drops were used, time instilled — documented in the record. For pediatric patients, cycloplegic dilation documentation complete.

Ophthalmology's prior authorization burden is driven primarily by anti-VEGF drugs (Eylea, Lucentis, Vabysmo, Beovu) and surgical procedures. Authorization management at scale — hundreds of patients on anti-VEGF with annual auth renewals — requires systematic tracking.

**Anti-VEGF authorization:**

Eylea (aflibercept): Requires diagnosis documentation (wet AMD, CRVO, BRVO, DME, myopic CNV) and visual acuity documentation meeting coverage criteria. Annual renewal typically required.

Lucentis (ranibizumab): Similar indications with diagnosis-specific coverage criteria.

Vabysmo (faricimab): Newer agent with prior authorization at most commercial plans. Step therapy may require failure of bevacizumab first at some payers.

Beovu (brolucizumab): Limited availability with specific coverage criteria related to prior treatment response.

Note: Bevacizumab (Avastin) is off-label and not subject to prior auth — widely used as the lower-cost alternative where payer policies permit.

**Authorization renewal management:**

For practices with 200-300 patients on Eylea or Lucentis, managing annual authorization renewal is a significant administrative burden. Renewals must be initiated 30-60 days before expiration to ensure continuous coverage. A lapse in authorization means either delaying treatment or an uncovered injection — either outcome is problematic.

**Surgical prior authorization:**

Cataract surgery authorization is generally straightforward but requires diagnosis documentation (best corrected visual acuity, functional impairment). Glaucoma surgery (trabeculectomy, tube shunt, MIGS) requires documentation of failed medical therapy. Pterygium, chalazion, and oculoplastic procedures have specific coverage criteria by payer.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Authorization status by patient by drug: Current auth, expiration date, renewal status. Renewal triggers 30-60 days before expiration. Surgical auth tracking: Pre-operative auth obtained before OR scheduling confirmed.

**Week 1-2: Configuration**

Cataract surgical pipeline built for your pre-op coordination workflow. Injection program dashboard configured for your drug mix and intervals. Diagnostic testing workflow built for your technician team. Dilation tracking configured for your exam room layout.

**Week 3: Training and Go-Live**

Technician team training on testing queue and dilation tracking. Front desk training on injection scheduling and authorization management. Surgical coordinator training on cataract pipeline. Provider training on dashboards.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Cataract pipeline visibility immediate — no more day-of missing items. Dilation tracking active. Injection scheduling proactive.

Month 3: Cataract volume measurably increasing. Injection compliance rate improving. Testing workflow efficiency measurable in patients-seen-per-session.

Month 6: 15-20% surgical volume increase documented. Zero missed injection appointments. Authorization management systematic with no lapses.

**Investment:**

• Professional: $499/month — Full suite including cataract coordination, injection scheduling, testing workflow, dilation tracking, authorization management
• Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: How does the cataract surgical pipeline work?
faqs.a: Every patient scheduled for cataract surgery has a pre-operative checklist — biometry completed, IOL selected and ordered, medical clearance obtained, facility scheduling confirmed, implant confirmation received, pre-op instructions sent, consent signed. Cases with incomplete checklists are flagged before the surgical schedule is finalized. The practice never discovers a missing IOL on surgery morning.

faqs.q: How do you manage 300 patients on anti-VEGF injection schedules?
faqs.a: The injection program dashboard shows every patient with their drug, last injection date, interval, next due date, and scheduling status. Color-coded status (on schedule, due soon, overdue) makes priorities visible. Patients approaching their due date without an appointment generate outreach. Authorization status per drug per patient is tracked with renewals triggered 30-60 days before expiration.

When dilation drops are instilled, the time is recorded in clinIQ. The provider dashboard shows each dilated patient with elapsed dilation time. When the dilation window completes, the provider receives a notification — 'Room 4 ready.' Providers see which rooms are ready for fundus exam vs. which patients are still dilating, allowing optimal sequencing of patients.

Protocol-based testing orders are generated automatically based on visit type — glaucoma follow-up triggers visual fields and OCT RNFL; macular degeneration monitoring triggers OCT macula. Technicians see a prioritized testing queue showing who needs what tests in what order. Results are automatically available when the provider opens the encounter.

faqs.q: How do you handle Eylea and Lucentis prior authorization renewals?
faqs.a: Authorization expiration dates are tracked per patient per drug. clinIQ triggers renewal requests 30-60 days before expiration — enough lead time to receive approval before the patient's next injection appointment. Renewal submission uses templates auto-populated with current diagnosis, visual acuity, and treatment history. No injection is delayed because of an expired authorization.

faqs.q: Does clinIQ work with ASC-based cataract practices?
faqs.a: Yes. Facility-specific scheduling is coordinated within the surgical pipeline — ASC block time, case type requirements, and facility pre-admission requirements are tracked alongside the clinical pre-op checklist. Practices with multiple ASC relationships manage each facility's scheduling requirements separately within the same system.

faqs.q: How does bilateral injection coordination work?
faqs.a: Patients with AMD or diabetic macular edema in both eyes often need injections in both eyes on the same visit. clinIQ coordinates bilateral injection scheduling — both eyes treated on the same day, documentation for each eye complete and billed separately. Authorization is tracked per eye per drug.

faqs.q: How long until we see volume improvement?
faqs.a: Surgical volume improvement is typically measurable by month 2-3 — practices report 10-20% volume increases once cataract pipeline coordination eliminates day-of cancellations and pre-op delays. Injection compliance improvement is immediate — proactive scheduling outreach begins day one and patients who would have missed appointments are captured.

cta.headline: See how ophthalmology practices increase surgical volume and eliminate missed injection appointments
cta.subhead: 15-minute demo. Cataract pipeline coordination, anti-VEGF injection scheduling, testing workflow, dilation-aware patient flow.

testimonial.quote: We increased cataract volume 17% without adding staff — purely from better pre-op coordination. The injection program management ended the constant fire drill around scheduling.
